question:
Let $G$ be a group with identity $e$ and $\phi:G\rightarrow G$
a function such that
\[\phi(g_1)\phi(g_2)\phi(g_3)=\phi(h_1)\phi(h_2)\phi(h_3)\]
whenever $g_1g_2g_3=e=h_1h_2h_3$.  Prove that there exists an element
$a\in G$ such that $\psi(x)=a\phi(x)$ is a homomorphism (i.e.
$\psi(xy)=\psi(x)\psi(y)$ for all $x,y\in G$).
solution:
In order to have $\psi(x) = a \phi(x)$ for all $x$, we must in
particular have this for $x = e$, and so we take $a = \phi(e)^{-1}$.
We first note that
\[
\phi(g) \phi(e) \phi(g^{-1}) = \phi(e) \phi(g) \phi(g^{-1})
\]
and so $\phi(g)$ commutes with $\phi(e)$ for all $g$. Next, we note that
\[
\phi(x) \phi(y) \phi(y^{-1}x^{-1}) = \phi(e) \phi(xy) \phi(y^{-1}x^{-1})
\]
and using the commutativity of $\phi(e)$, we deduce
\[
\phi(e)^{-1} \phi(x) \phi(e)^{-1} \phi(y) = \phi(e)^{-1} \phi(xy)
\]
or $\psi(xy) = \psi(x) \psi(y)$, as desired.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $groupwhole$ be a group with identity $e$ and $mysteryfunc:groupwhole\rightarrow groupwhole$
a function such that
\[
mysteryfunc(gfirst)mysteryfunc(gsecond)mysteryfunc(gthird)=mysteryfunc(hfirst)mysteryfunc(hsecond)mysteryfunc(hthird)
\]
whenever $gfirst gsecond gthird=e=hfirst hsecond hthird$.  Prove that there exists an element
$shiftconst\in groupwhole$ such that $adjustfunc(xinvar)=shiftconst mysteryfunc(xinvar)$ is a homomorphism (i.e.
$adjustfunc(xinvar yinvar)=adjustfunc(xinvar)adjustfunc(yinvar)$ for all $xinvar,yinvar\in groupwhole$).
descriptive_long.solution:
In order to have $adjustfunc(xinvar) = shiftconst mysteryfunc(xinvar)$ for all $xinvar$, we must in
particular have this for $xinvar = e$, and so we take $shiftconst = mysteryfunc(e)^{-1}$.
We first note that
\[
mysteryfunc(gvarbl) \, mysteryfunc(e) \, mysteryfunc(gvarbl^{-1}) = mysteryfunc(e) \, mysteryfunc(gvarbl) \, mysteryfunc(gvarbl^{-1})
\]
and so $mysteryfunc(gvarbl)$ commutes with $mysteryfunc(e)$ for all $gvarbl$. Next, we note that
\[
mysteryfunc(xinvar) \, mysteryfunc(yinvar) \, mysteryfunc(yinvar^{-1} xinvar^{-1}) = mysteryfunc(e) \, mysteryfunc(xinvar yinvar) \, mysteryfunc(yinvar^{-1} xinvar^{-1})
\]
and using the commutativity of $mysteryfunc(e)$, we deduce
\[
mysteryfunc(e)^{-1} \, mysteryfunc(xinvar) \, mysteryfunc(e)^{-1} \, mysteryfunc(yinvar) = mysteryfunc(e)^{-1} \, mysteryfunc(xinvar yinvar)
\]
or $adjustfunc(xinvar yinvar) = adjustfunc(xinvar) \, adjustfunc(yinvar)$, as desired.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $blueprint$ be a group with identity $e$ and $crosswind:blueprint\rightarrow blueprint$
a function such that
\[
crosswind(drumstick)crosswind(photograph)crosswind(rainforest)=crosswind(buttercup)crosswind(watermelon)crosswind(quicksand)
\]
whenever $drumstickphotographrainforest=e=buttercupwatermelonquicksand$.  Prove that there exists an element
$landscape\in blueprint$ such that $silhouette(pineapple)=landscape crosswind(pineapple)$ is a homomorphism (i.e.
$silhouette(pineapple tombstone)=silhouette(pineapple)silhouette(tombstone)$ for all $pineapple,tombstone\in blueprint$).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
In order to have $silhouette(pineapple) = landscape crosswind(pineapple)$ for all $pineapple$, we must in
particular have this for $pineapple = e$, and so we take $landscape = crosswind(e)^{-1}$.
We first note that
\[
crosswind(sandcastle) crosswind(e) crosswind(sandcastle^{-1}) = crosswind(e) crosswind(sandcastle) crosswind(sandcastle^{-1})
\]
and so $crosswind(sandcastle)$ commutes with $crosswind(e)$ for all $sandcastle$. Next, we note that
\[
crosswind(pineapple) crosswind(tombstone) crosswind(tombstone^{-1}pineapple^{-1}) = crosswind(e) crosswind(pineapple tombstone) crosswind(tombstone^{-1}pineapple^{-1})
\]
and using the commutativity of $crosswind(e)$, we deduce
\[
crosswind(e)^{-1} crosswind(pineapple) crosswind(e)^{-1} crosswind(tombstone) = crosswind(e)^{-1} crosswind(pineapple tombstone)
\]
or $silhouette(pineapple tombstone) = silhouette(pineapple) silhouette(tombstone)$, as desired.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $unordered$ be a group with identity $nonentity$ and $antiimage:unordered\rightarrow unordered$
a function such that
\[
antiimage(outsidera)antiimage(outsiderb)antiimage(outsiderc)=antiimage(strangera)antiimage(strangerb)antiimage(strangerc)
\]
whenever $outsideraoutsiderboutsiderc=nonentity=strangerastrangerbstrangerc$.  Prove that there exists an element
$constant\in unordered$ such that $distorter(nonmember)=constant\,antiimage(nonmember)$ is a homomorphism (i.e.
$distorter(nonmember\,foreigner)=distorter(nonmember)distorter(foreigner)$ for all $nonmember,foreigner\in unordered$).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
In order to have $distorter(nonmember) = constant\,antiimage(nonmember)$ for all $nonmember$, we must in
particular have this for $nonmember = nonentity$, and so we take $constant = antiimage(nonentity)^{-1}$.
We first note that
\[
antiimage(antiunit) \, antiimage(nonentity) \, antiimage(antiunit^{-1}) = antiimage(nonentity) \, antiimage(antiunit) \, antiimage(antiunit^{-1})
\]
and so $antiimage(antiunit)$ commutes with $antiimage(nonentity)$ for all $antiunit$. Next, we note that
\[
antiimage(nonmember) \, antiimage(foreigner) \, antiimage(foreigner^{-1}nonmember^{-1}) = antiimage(nonentity) \, antiimage(nonmember\,foreigner) \, antiimage(foreigner^{-1}nonmember^{-1})
\]
and using the commutativity of $antiimage(nonentity)$, we deduce
\[
antiimage(nonentity)^{-1} \, antiimage(nonmember) \, antiimage(nonentity)^{-1} \, antiimage(foreigner) = antiimage(nonentity)^{-1} \, antiimage(nonmember\,foreigner)
\]
or $distorter(nonmember\,foreigner) = distorter(nonmember) \, distorter(foreigner)$, as desired.

garbled_string.question:
Let $sdhgrlta$ be a group with identity $mxcvplod$ and $\lqkngwaz:sdhgrlta\rightarrow sdhgrlta$
a function such that
\[\lqkngwaz(qzxwvtnp)\lqkngwaz(hjgrksla)\lqkngwaz(bmvcltqe)=\lqkngwaz(pzkrdmqc)\lqkngwaz(tfhyznwa)\lqkngwaz(nlsrjdop)\]
whenever $qzxwvtnp hjgrksla bmvcltqe=mxcvplod=pzkrdmqc tfhyznwa nlsrjdop$.  Prove that there exists an element
$flrpbqsn\in sdhgrlta$ such that $\kdrjtwqm(vjbqslrk)=flrpbqsn\lqkngwaz(vjbqslrk)$ is a homomorphism (i.e.
$\kdrjtwqm(vjbqslrk zmndplfh)=\kdrjtwqm(vjbqslrk)\kdrjtwqm(zmndplfh)$ for all $vjbqslrk,zmndplfh\in sdhgrlta$).
garbled_string.solution:
In order to have $\kdrjtwqm(vjbqslrk) = flrpbqsn \lqkngwaz(vjbqslrk)$ for all $vjbqslrk$, we must in
particular have this for $vjbqslrk = mxcvplod$, and so we take $flrpbqsn = \lqkngwaz(mxcvplod)^{-1}$.
We first note that
\[
\lqkngwaz(kwtrslvn) \lqkngwaz(mxcvplod) \lqkngwaz(kwtrslvn^{-1}) = \lqkngwaz(mxcvplod) \lqkngwaz(kwtrslvn) \lqkngwaz(kwtrslvn^{-1})
\]
and so $\lqkngwaz(kwtrslvn)$ commutes with $\lqkngwaz(mxcvplod)$ for all $kwtrslvn$. Next, we note that
\[
\lqkngwaz(vjbqslrk) \lqkngwaz(zmndplfh) \lqkngwaz(zmndplfh^{-1}vjbqslrk^{-1}) = \lqkngwaz(mxcvplod) \lqkngwaz(vjbqslrk zmndplfh) \lqkngwaz(zmndplfh^{-1}vjbqslrk^{-1})
\]
and using the commutativity of $\lqkngwaz(mxcvplod)$, we deduce
\[
\lqkngwaz(mxcvplod)^{-1} \lqkngwaz(vjbqslrk) \lqkngwaz(mxcvplod)^{-1} \lqkngwaz(zmndplfh) = \lqkngwaz(mxcvplod)^{-1} \lqkngwaz(vjbqslrk zmndplfh)
\]
or $\kdrjtwqm(vjbqslrk zmndplfh) = \kdrjtwqm(vjbqslrk) \kdrjtwqm(zmndplfh)$, as desired.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $G$ be a group with identity element $e$, and let $H$ be 
any (possibly different) group.  Suppose a map $\varphi:G\to H$ satisfies

\[\varphi(g_1)\varphi(g_2)\varphi(g_3)\varphi(g_4)
      =\varphi(h_1)\varphi(h_2)\varphi(h_3)\varphi(h_4)\tag{\*}\]
whenever the four-fold products in $G$ obey
$g_1g_2g_3g_4=h_1h_2h_3h_4=e$.  

Prove that there is an element $a\in H$ such that the map
$\psi:G\to H$ defined by $\displaystyle \psi(x)=a\,\varphi(x)$ is a
homomorphism, i.e.
$\psi(xy)=\psi(x)\psi(y)$ for every $x,y\in G$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write c = \varphi (e) \in  H.

Step 1.  Choosing the multiplier.
For \psi (x) = a \varphi (x) to satisfy \psi (e) = e_H, we must have
  e_H = \psi (e) = a \varphi (e) = a c,
hence we take
  a = c^{-1} = \varphi (e)^{-1}.

Step 2.  c is central in the image of \varphi .
Compare in (*) the two quadruples
    (g, g^{-1}, e, e)
    (g, e, g^{-1}, e)
for any g \in  G.  Both products equal e in G, so (*) gives
    \varphi (g) \varphi (g^{-1}) c c = \varphi (g) c \varphi (g^{-1}) c.
Right-multiply by c^{-1} to obtain
    \varphi (g) \varphi (g^{-1}) c = \varphi (g) c \varphi (g^{-1}).
Left-multiply by \varphi (g)^{-1} to get
    \varphi (g^{-1}) c = c \varphi (g^{-1}).
Since {g^{-1}: g \in  G} = G, it follows that
    c \varphi (x) = \varphi (x) c   for every x \in  G.

Step 3.  A key product relation.
Apply (*) to the quadruples
    (x, y, y^{-1} x^{-1}, e)
    (e, x y, y^{-1} x^{-1}, e)
for any x,y \in  G.  Both products equal e, so
    \varphi (x) \varphi (y) \varphi (y^{-1} x^{-1}) c
  = c \varphi (x y) \varphi (y^{-1} x^{-1}) c.
Right-multiply by c^{-1} and use the centrality of c:
    \varphi (x) \varphi (y) \varphi (y^{-1} x^{-1})
  = c \varphi (x y) \varphi (y^{-1} x^{-1}).
Cancel \varphi (y^{-1} x^{-1}) on the right (valid in H) to conclude
    \varphi (x) \varphi (y) = c \varphi (x y).
Because c is central this is equivalent to \varphi (x) \varphi (y) = \varphi (x y) c.

Step 4.  Verifying the homomorphism property.
Using \varphi (x) \varphi (y) = c \varphi (x y) and the centrality of c:
  \psi (x) \psi (y)
    = c^{-1} \varphi (x) \cdot  c^{-1} \varphi (y)
    = c^{-2} \varphi (x) \varphi (y)
    = c^{-2} \cdot  c \varphi (x y)
    = c^{-1} \varphi (x y)
    = \psi (x y).
Thus \psi  is a homomorphism G \to  H.

Conclusion.
Taking a = \varphi (e)^{-1} makes \psi (x) = a \varphi (x) a group homomorphism, as required.

core_steps: Pick a = φ(e)^{-1} so that ψ(x)=aφ(x) satisfies ψ(e)=e.
From the equality for triples (g,e,g^{-1}) and (e,g,g^{-1}), deduce that φ(e) commutes with every φ(g).
Apply the triple–equality to (x, y, y^{-1}x^{-1}) versus (e, xy, y^{-1}x^{-1}); use the centrality of φ(e) to conclude ψ(xy)=ψ(x)ψ(y).

slot1.description: Codomain of the map φ (need only be a group, not necessarily the same as the domain).

slot2.description: Number of factors in the hypothesis (any fixed integer ≥3 works, since extra identity elements can pad the needed decompositions).
